local_names.malay: Nasi Goreng Daging Lembu dan Kentang

aliases: Fried Rice with Beef and Potato
Nasi Goreng Daging Lembu dan Kentang

english: Fried Rice with Beef and Potato
malay: Nasi Goreng Daging Lembu dan Kentang

region_of_origin: China (Lanzhou / Northwest Chinese tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific beef-and-potato fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried rice as a category; no specific beef-and-potato variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for beef and potato fried rice. Generic food concepts in SNOMED CT are insufficiently granular for this dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of beef and potato fried rice is approximately 400 g, equivalent to a single plate as typically served in a Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fried rice with meat (SR Legacy #21038) and component-level data for beef, potato, and egg; values weighted and adapted for this dish composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for beef-and-potato fried rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy fried rice with meat (FDC ID 21038, energy 163 kcal/100g, protein 5.0g, fat 4.5g, carbs 25.0g) with adjustments for potato content (approximately 15% by weight, contributing additional starch and potassium) and beef content (approximately 20% by weight, contributing additional protein and fat). Sodium estimated from soy sauce seasoning typical of Chinese fried rice. Dietary fibre estimated from potato and vegetable components. All values represent a best-estimate composite per 100g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked) and mixed rice dishes; Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef-and-potato fried rice. GI estimated at 65 based on the GI of cooked white rice (GI ~64–72, mean ~68 per University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward slightly for the presence of potato (GI ~56–65 for boiled potato), beef protein (protein and fat slow gastric emptying), and egg content. The dominant glycemic driver is the white rice component (~55–60% of dish weight). A GI of 65 (medium category) is a defensible analogous estimate consistent with published GI values for mixed rice dishes with protein and fat. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (65 × 23.5 × 4.0) / 100 = 61.1 g, rounded to 60.8 g using serving-adjusted carbohydrate content of 94 g per 400 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving provides approximately 1680 mg sodium, representing 73–84% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size to 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 61 per standard serving and a medium GI of 65, this dish can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to half a plate (200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 6.4 g saturated fat and 1680 mg sodium per standard serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean beef cuts, request reduced-sodium soy sauce, and limit serving frequency to occasional consumption within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef and Potato Fried Rice (牛肉土豆炒饭, niú ròu tǔdòu chǎofàn) is a Chinese stir-fried rice dish combining cooked white rice, sliced or minced beef, diced potato, egg, garlic, spring onion, and soy sauce, all tossed together in a hot wok with vegetable oil. The dish is associated with Northwest Chinese culinary traditions, particularly Lanzhou-style cooking, where potato is a common starch accompaniment to beef. The texture is a combination of fluffy rice grains, tender beef pieces, and slightly crisp potato cubes, with a savoury umami flavour profile from soy sauce and wok hei (the characteristic smoky aroma from high-heat wok cooking). In Singapore, this dish is served in Chinese restaurants and noodle houses, often as a rice alternative on menus that also feature Lanzhou beef noodles. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g restaurant serving provides approximately 672 kcal, 24.8 g protein, 20.4 g fat, and 94 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high at approximately 1680 mg per serving due to soy sauce seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is high per serving owing to the white rice base, which is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The potato component contributes dietary fibre, potassium, and vitamin C relative to plain fried rice. The dish contains soy and egg allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for beef-and-potato fried rice was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried rice with meat data adapted for the specific ingredient composition of this dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the dominant ingredient (white rice) has well-established nutritional and GI data.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy fried rice source data and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values based on USDA component data. Sodium value of 420 mg/100g reflects typical soy-sauce-seasoned Chinese fried rice; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly depending on seasoning practice. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains beef and the halal certification status of the specific restaurant (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles) is not confirmed in the source data; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify with the restaurant. The GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date.
